method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The Japanese name Nishinoshima is glossed as “western island”; the island lies about 130 km west of Chichijima in the Ogasawara Islands.[2][3] It was first recorded by the Spanish ship Rosario in 1702 and was named Rosario Island before later being renamed Nishinoshima.[3] The name is also sometimes romanized as Nishino-shima.[5]

==Geology==
Nishinoshima is an active volcano in the Ogasawara Islands, about 1,000 km south of Tokyo and 130 km west of Chichijima, on the Shichito-Iwojima Ridge of the Izu-Bonin arc.[4] Its volcanism is related to subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Philippine Sea Plate along the Ogasawara Trench.[4] The island is the emergent summit of a large submarine volcano about 30 km in diameter, with additional submarine peaks to the west and south and a possible satellite cone on the northeast flank.[1][4]

The pre-eruption island was described as about 650 m long, 200 m wide, 77,000 m2 in area, and 25 m high, with reefs on its northeast side.[1] Nishinoshima was historically characterized as an andesitic volcanic island, although later eruptions showed a wider compositional range.[1][3] Ejecta from the 1973–1974 activity had SiO2 contents of 51.8–60.1 wt%.[1]

The first recorded eruption began in May 1973 on the seafloor about 400 m east of the southern tip of the island and produced a new island by September of that year.[1] The eruption began with submarine phreatomagmatic explosions, migrated semicircularly, and involved five craters that emitted volcanic blocks and lava flows.[1] Although erosion removed much of the new volcanic edifices, redeposited material connected the old and new islands, and by 1982 sediment movement had closed the mouth of the intervening bay.[1]

The 2013–2020 eruptive period has been divided into four episodes, with durations changing from about two years in Episode 1 to 120 days in Episode 2, about one week in Episode 3, and 270 days in Episode 4.[3] Estimated average effusion rates were 2.0 × 105 m3/day for the 2013–2015 eruption and 1.5 × 105 m3/day for the 2017 eruption.[3] Episode 4 began with Strombolian activity and lava effusion, progressed to lava-fountain eruptions, and ended with ash emission likely produced by phreatomagmatic activity.[4]

Petrological study of submarine deposits from the 2020 eruption found basalt, basaltic andesite, andesite, and dacite magmas.[3] The andesites were similar to those erupted from Nishinoshima since 1973, and their primary magmas are interpreted to originate directly from the mantle through shallow, hydrous melting of plagioclase peridotite.[3] The 2020 basalts resemble older basalts from surrounding submarine knolls, suggesting that basaltic magmas may have been produced throughout Nishinoshima’s history but reached the island summit as an eruption center in 2020.[3]

==Ecology==
Because Nishinoshima is an isolated oceanic island within the Ogasawara archipelago, its ecology is studied in the broader context of the islands’ high endemism, island-specific ecosystems, and biota derived from multiple source regions including Oceania, Southeast Asia, and Japan.[5] The Ogasawara Islands are regarded as a natural setting for observing adaptive radiation, speciation, and ecological succession in isolation.[3][5] Nishinoshima is especially valuable for studying early colonization because volcanic disturbance repeatedly resets terrestrial habitats, allowing researchers to observe the establishment of life on newly exposed surfaces.[2][3] Botanical studies have documented plant invasion on Nishinoshima’s new volcanic land, and common purslane, ''Portulaca oleracea'', has been among the species investigated in relation to the island’s vegetation.[4] The island’s flora is vulnerable to eruptions, and after researchers collected living purslane plants before a 2019 eruption, the plant was later reported to have disappeared from Nishinoshima itself.[2] Continuing changes caused by volcanic deposition and marine erosion make ecological succession on the island unstable compared with older and more established Ogasawara ecosystems.[5] Marine ecosystems around Nishinoshima, including coral reef development, are also monitored as part of research into how biodiversity becomes established around newly formed or altered volcanic islands.[3]
